subtitle: The Evolution of Despotism, from Mao to Now
title: The Patient Dictator
description: The Evolution of Despotism, from Mao to Now
body_html:
The China Mao grew up in was feudal, poor, and run by a king who was run by a queen who, if anything, proved to be a ruinous despot. By his nineteenth birthday, he had successfully partnered with Sun Yat-sen, a Christian dissident, in the Xinhai Revolution. Their outfit was called Tongmenghui and it was just one of several that participated. The uprising lasted four months and managed to pull China out of the Qing stranglehold but did little to bring about the promised change to the status quo, even though the subjects were now citizens. Yuan Shikai, the leader of the new Republic of China, was himself a monarchist. This is when Mao discovered socialism.

In 1919, Tongmenghui merged with an even older Xingzhonghui to form a new entity called Kuomintang. Xingzhonghui and Tongmenghui shared the same founder, Sun Yat-sen. Two years later, Chen Duxiu and Li Dazhao floated the Communist Party of China in Shanghai and Mao joined right away. In 1925, Sun died and Kuomintang (KMT) went to Chiang Kai-shek. This is where we start witnessing a CPC–KMT rift thanks to Mao. His call to violence against the landowners didn’t sit well with KMT which enjoyed a generous membership from that group. From one such landowner family in Shaanxi came the thirteen-year-old Xi Zhongxun. One would expect Xi to hate Mao’s guts given the latter’s resentment against precisely his kind of family. But no, the boy went on to not only demonstrate extensively against Chiang’s nationalist regime but even join the CPC during one of his prison stints.

Schandillia is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Mao’s Launch and a New China

By 1933, China was already in a de facto split between the nationalists and the communists. Mao had declared an autonomous Jiangxi Soviet in the region under his control and even started issuing stamps and banknotes bearing Lenin’s picture. Xi and comrades Liu Zhidan and Gao Gang founded a similar soviet in Shaanxi. When KMT launched crackdowns against this “Chinese Soviet Republic” in 1934, the unprepared and outnumbered CSR leadership ordered an escape out of Jiangxi famously known as the Long March. More than 90% would perish before the end of the trek. This was a watershed moment in China’s history and it officially marked the beginning of Mao’s ascent to political stardom. The exercise was helmed by Mao and fellow rebel Zhou Enlai, who’d eventually go on to become Communist China’s first Premier.

Mao practically worshiped Stalin as the purest inheritor of Marxist ideals. That’s why when Stalin ordered Mao to drop his ego and collaborate with Chiang Kai-shek against the Japanese during the Second Sino-Japanese War in 1935, he did exactly that. This helped Mao too as it meant no more hiding from Chiang’s nationalist forces. The March finally came to an end with Mao and his men receiving food, shelter, and protection at Xi’s guerrilla base in Shaanxi. Gao and Mao struck a bond during their stay. Mao repaid his hospitality the same year when he intervened to rescue Xi, Gao, and Liu from execution in a local Leftist “rectification” exercise. These exercises were common in those days. By the end of the year, Mao was heading the CPC unopposed.

Mao’s rescuing, however, did not serve Liu much as he died the very next year fighting a republican warlord. 1937 witnessed the chilling episode we know today as the Rape of Nanking. Nevertheless, the Japanese left in 1940 and the CPC–KMT or communist–nationalist civil war resumed. On October 3, 1949, Mao proclaimed the People’s Republic of China from Tiananmen (lit. the Gate of Heavenly Peace) with Stalin’s support. Chiang Kai-shek and his KMT fled to Formosa (now Taiwan) where it continues to date as the Republic of China. In short, there were two Chinas now—a communist one on the mainland, and a nationalist one on the island.

In 1950, Mao launched his Land Reform Laws unleashing, in the name of enforcing it, a wave of terror to suppress dissent that lasted two years and left, by his own admission, close to half a million dead.1 Unofficial numbers run into the millions. The mandate to every village was to mark “at least one landlord for public execution.”2 That year, on Valentine’s Day, Mao and Stalin signed the Sino-Soviet Treaty of Friendship where Stalin pledged 300 million dollars in loans and military aid against a potential Japanese resurgence, something highly unlikely at the time given Japan’s near-absolute ruination in the World War, in exchange for Mao’s recognition of Mongolia’s independence.

A Rise and a Fall

Now let’s check on a good friend we haven’t heard of since the Long March of 1935. By the end of Mao’s purge program, Xi Zhongxun had been elected to helm the party’s propaganda. The very next year Mao launched his first five-year plan. This brought rapid collectivization and Chinese character simplification to improve literacy. Oh, and Stalin died.

A little over three months after Stalin’s death, Xi’s second wife Qi had her third child Xi. Stalin’s death was a body blow to Mao who had a personal distaste for Khruschev, the new Russian boss. It was also a major window of opportunity for elements hoping to break free from various communist regimes the world over. This included China. Away from Beijing, reprisals started brewing in Xinjiang. This is where Xi shone. In two years, with Mao’s support, he successfully negotiated political solutions to the social unrest and prevented any violent uprising. As a token of gratitude, the Dalai Lama visited Beijing and gifted Xi an Omega. When the Lama’s brother visited him again in the 1980s, Xi was still wearing that Omega. In 1954, Gao lost Mao’s grace over his personal rivalry with Zhou Enlai and committed suicide. The following year, he was posthumously purged from the CPC. Gao’s fall remains a mystery because no official reason was ever offered for his purge. All we have is speculations, one of which points to his ties with the Soviet Union.3

In 1956, Hungary rebelled against the Soviet Union and although crushed, managed to get Mao worried. As a preemptive strike against any dissent, Mao started the Hundred Flowers Campaign where feedback was solicited from the public for the first time ever. And feedback came. Overwhelmingly negative. The plan backfired so badly that Mao backtracked within months and began persecuting his critics. About half a million were incarcerated or executed. This is when the Sino-Soviet alliance started to visibly fall apart.

A lot happened that year. Xi made it to the CPC Central Committee, Khruschev delivered his “Secret Speech” and kick-started USSR’s de-Stalinization, and Mao denounced this as an unacceptable Marxist revisionism. The rift was now very pronounced. In 1958, Mao launched his second five-year plan, the notorious Great Leap Forward. This was a resounding disaster that left millions dead. Smaller farms were merged into large communes and livestock and farm implements were brought under collective ownership. A large farm workforce was diverted into steel production. Typhoons, floods, and droughts ushered in the Great Chinese Famine that left between twenty-three and fifty-five million dead. Such was the desperation that people resorted to eating pets, rodents, and—even people. As China descended into disarray, Xi ascended to vice-premiership under Zhou Enlai. By April 1959, Mao’s Leap had proven so unpopular that he handed over the Chairmanship to a more moderate Liu Shaoqi.

The Leap finally folded in 1962, a year before its actual due date. Meanwhile, remember Liu Zhidan who along with Xi and Gao had sheltered Mao in Shaanxi after the Long March and who later died fighting a republican warlord? Well, someone wrote a biography on him. This was problematic. Although Liu was a martyr in CPC books, Gao wasn’t. And the biography had some rather flattering references to the latter. This wasn’t good for Xi who was seen as supportive of the book. This made him appear “anti-party.” Spearheaded by Kang Sheng, a security and intelligence official with the CPC, the move against Xi gained momentum through 1965 when he was finally purged from all leadership roles and sent to work at a tractor factory in Luoyang as deputy manager.

Meanwhile, wounded by the erosion of relevance after the Leap debacle, Mao was desperate for a comeback to power. But that called for a cult of personality which wasn’t easy in a system where the collective trumped the individual. Mao was old and was seen by many as weak and cranky. If he were to make a comeback, he needed a titanic image overhaul. So he came up with an idea.

On July 16, 1966, Mao took a large contingent of cameramen to Wuhan to participate in an annual swimming event. With a reported five thousand in attendance, Mao plunged into the Yangtze in a highly publicized theater of strength and vitality; the most absurd piece of propaganda. He covered a little over nine miles in a little over one hour, according to reports in the Chinese media, almost twice as fast as the current 1500m freestyle record-holder Sun Yang. The idea worked. It made him an overnight sensation and propelled him back into reckoning. Did he really swim that far or that fast? Of course not. But good propaganda rendered that immaterial. Optics fixed, Mao launched the Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution, the second darkest chapter in China’s history. The stated purpose was to rid China of all capitalistic elements and purging of the Four Olds—old ideas, old culture, old customs, and old habits.

Despite its cultural connotations, the so-called revolution was merely Mao’s way of eliminating political rivals and further crystallizing his image as the supreme leader. His ideas were published in a Little Red Book and copies were distributed among students who were banded into vigilante groups called the Red Guards. These Red Guards were given a free hand to go after the evil bourgeoisie. This “going after” included the burning of books, destruction of museums and temples, summary trials and murders of teachers, and by some accounts, even ritualistic cannibalism. Intellectuals were anti-revolutionary as they could “think for themselves,” so schools and universities were shut down. This vigilantism plus large-scale purges meant millions perished. This included Liu Shaoqi, the third most powerful man in all of China at the time.

At the height of this Cultural Revolution, the Red Guards ransacked Xi’s family home, and his wife was forced to publicly denounce him for his support of Liu’s biography. The episode drove one of their daughters, Xi Heping, into suicide.4 Remember that kid who was born to Xi and Qi three months after Stalin died? Well, he was thirteen by now, his schooling cut short by the Revolution. In 1968, Xi was jailed as Mao launched the Down to the Countryside Movement to prevent the Red Guards from growing too strong. Theoretically, the movement was a program for all educated youths or zhiqing to spend time in the countryside far from the comforts of their families to get re-educated by the peasants and help develop the hinterland. Mao’s insecurity wasn’t entirely unfounded though. By 1968, his Cultural Movement had started showing signs of fatigue and infighting had become a common occurrence among the Red Guards. One episode that summer involved Beijing’s Qinghua University.

On Mao’s orders, an army of thirty thousand factory workers managed to crush a bloody brawl between two university factions. As a reward, Mao sent them a basket of mangoes he’d earlier received as a gift from a Pakistani Minister. Nobody in China had tasted the fruit before. Needless to say, the mangoes became an instant cult hit and emblematic of Mao’s “generosity.” Ironically, while this basket of mangoes helped reaffirm a despot in China, another would help end one in Pakistan, some twenty years later. The Down to the Countryside Movement was essentially to disperse the vigilantes all over China. The reason given was a less intimidating “reform through labor” but few were under any illusion about its true objectives. In fact, within a year of this launch, Mao declared an end to the Cultural Revolution, although the end wouldn’t truly arrive until his death seven years later.

The Patient Dictator

Now let’s return to Xi junior, the debased comrade’s eponymous son. The boy was also a victim of the Countryside Movement. He was sent to the Liangjiahe Village in the Shaanxi province to work as a party secretary. This is where his father had once given refuge to Mao after the Long March and set up one of the first Soviets of a proto-Communist China. The same Mao who now had his father purged and imprisoned. Here, the boy lived in a frugal cave house. The country life and the accompanying hardships were too much for the teenager and he fled to Beijing after just a few months, only to be caught and sent back to dig ditches at a labor camp as a disgraced “deserter.” These were exceptionally hard times.

At this point, let’s take a pause and step into the boy’s shoes. What do we feel about the regime? What are the dominant emotions? Resentment? Bitterness? Fury? Well, things were a tad different with this one. He went Redder than Red, a well-calculated move. In 1971, a battered but determined fifteen-year-old Xi junior joined the Communist Youth League of China. After being rejected seven times. And didn’t stop there. Over the next three years, he made no fewer than nine failed attempts to join the CPC, finally making it on the tenth. Daddy Xi continued to suffer extended incarcerations throughout the Revolution.

Then came 1976. Mao died and with him, so did the Revolution. In Mao: The Unknown Story, Jung Chang and Jon Halliday claim that as many as three million lost their lives. Others claim even more. The post-Mao era brought in a welcome whiff of moderation, not unlike the post-Stalin era in the Soviet Union. China was now in Deng Xiaoping’s hands. Daddy Xi was fully rehabilitated at the Third Plenary Session of the 11th CPC Central Committee in Dec 1978 and sent to Guangdong as a provincial secretary.

When Xi arrived, people were leaving Guangdong for neighboring Hong Kong en masse in pursuit of wages that were about a hundred times higher. Xi came up with the idea to liberalize the economy and with Deng’s support, established China’s first Special Economic Zones. By September 1982, he had risen up the ranks and gotten elected to the Politburo and the party secretariat. Three months later, a new constitution was adopted to prevent a repeat of Mao-like tyranny. It introduced a new concept to the nation’s politics—term limits. No President could stay in power for more than two consecutive five-year terms anymore.

Meanwhile, Xi junior continued to rise up the ranks at his own pace, independently of his father. In 1997, at the age of forty-four, he was named, along with 150 others, an alternate member of the 15th Central Committee of the CPC. Even at this point, he suffered discrimination for being born to a party senior, a princeling. In 2000, he became the governor of Fujian and two years later, a full member of the 16th Central Committee, marking his ascension to the national stage. This milestone, however, accompanied a great personal setback, the passing of his father. The son must’ve drawn solace in the fact that Xi Zhongxun died an honorable party member at the ripe old age of eighty-nine and received a distinguished State funeral.

In those years, one Chinese city was quickly becoming a quintessence of Chinese prosperity—Shanghai. Under mayor Chen Liangyu the city got a brand new Yangshan Port and saw its subway expanded. Chen was also behind Shanghai’s successful bid for the 2010 World Expo. He worked in Shanghai his whole life, but that didn’t limit the power he held as the party’s top honcho in a city this big and significant. Then something happened in 2006. One of China’s highest-level corruption investigations saw Chen implicated. And purged. And Xi junior, now almost fifty, was picked to replace him. This is when he came under serious national radar. His career was about to blast off from here because this upgrade came just in time for the 17th Party Congress in 2007. This convention was crucial to not only Xi but also to China.

Hu Jintao was set to retire in 2013. This convention was his succession planning exercise. This is where Xi managed to worm his way into the nine-member Politburo Standing Committee of the CPC. How he pulled that off, we don’t know. China is quite opaque like that. Do note that he managed to outrank Li Keqiang, one of the most powerful persons in China at the time, despite the latter being endorsed by President Hu himself. This is seen as a veritable diplomatic masterstroke, one that almost cleared Xi’s path for 2013.

Early next year Xi was elected the Vice President of China, further cementing his career trajectory as the future Paramount Leader. The following four years were spent traveling the world preparing for his future role at the helm. One such trip was to Mexico on Feb 11, 2008. Addressing a gathering of Chinese expats, he castigated the “well-fed foreign critics” for defaming China. This speech established Xi in the international reckoning, further crystallizing his odds. Then came October 2012. The 18th National Party Congress. This was the moment of truth. Xi Jinping was officially named to succeed Hu Jintao as China’s President in a few months. In this capacity, Xi received three roles:

  1. President, People’s Republic of China,

  2. Chairman, Central Military Commission, and

  3. General Secretary, Communist Party of China.

The writing was on the wall. Immediately upon taking charge, Jinping launched an unremitting crackdown on corruption that touched well over a million government officials, several high-ranking ones included. In reality, this was also a party “rectification” campaign, a move to purge rivals. Through this exercise, Xi managed to get powerful princelings and seniors like Zhou Yongkang, Sun Zhengcai, and Bo Xilai eliminated. This already made him the most powerful man in China. But that wasn’t enough. All of this was to go away eventually.

Remember that new constitution Xi Jinping’s father had helped push in 1982 to prevent another Mao. It had one key element added to this effect—term limits. No matter how powerful, Xi just couldn’t go beyond ten years. This, he fixed in 2018 at the 13th National People’s Congress. It was meant to be a succession planning huddle like the one in 2007. However, instead of appointing a potential successor, Xi pulled a coup. He introduced a constitutional amendment bill to abolish term limits. It passed. Almost without dissent. Along with the removal of term limits, the amendment also enshrined into the Constitution Xi’s political philosophy, the Xi Jinping Thought. The last time this was done was when Mao lived (recall the Little Red Book). This move effectively placed Xi in the pantheon of the world’s uncontested. But this is where Xi departed from the road taken by Mao and Stalin. Optics.

All the censorship and crackdowns notwithstanding, Xi has since meticulously crafted the image of a leader drunk not on power but on China’s upliftment. He’s ruthless, but not seen as such. He’s despotic, but not feared as such. At least not by the average Chinese. While his predecessors cultivated fear and trepidation, Jinping has cultivated progress as his pet obsession. Gaudy cities, glittering skyscapes, lightning-fast trains—he has given his proletariat something to feel proud of. And pride often takes attention away from the erosion of rights and liberties. Even if the trains don’t run on capacity and even if the cities remain uninhabited.

So that’s the story of the world’s most patient dictator. Who didn’t let humiliation fill him with vengeance, vengeance cloud his vision, and vision blind him to his predecessors’ realities. If tyranny is an art, Xi has come closer to mastering it than anyone ever could.

References

1

Stavis, Benedict. “Postliberation Policy on Mechanization.” The Politics of Agricultural Mechanization in China, Cornell University Press, USA, 1978, pp. 28–29.

2

Barnett, A. Doak, and Ezra Vogel. “The Government in County X: Organization and Operation.” Cadres, Bureaucracy, and Political Power in Communist China, Columbia University Press, USA, 1967, p. 228.

3

Teiwes, Frederick C. “5. The Purge of Gao Gang and Rao Shushi.” Politics and Purges in China: Rectification and the Decline of Party Norms, 1950-1965, 2nd ed., M. E. Sharpe, USA, 1993, p. 130.

4

Chen, Alfred L. “Childhood and Youth: Privilege and Trauma, 1953–1979.” Xi Jinping: Political Career, Governance, and Leadership, 1953–2018, Oxford University Press, USA, 2022, p. 25.

publication.name: Schandillia
publication.hero_text: Thrilling, well-referenced dives into Indian and global history—mostly Indian—with a dash of serious myth-busting, that challenges what you always held as self-evident. In short, history with the rigor of science.

comments.body: China reached it’s peak by the time Xi come to power. India has a chance of getting to closer to China in terms of potential, that is knowledge and Skill. We lack  Leadership and Investment. I made guess in 2010-2012 , while I made trips to China, that it is inevitable for India to follow the China’s growth path, in 20 years to come, due to Market Potential. But we couldn’t get there.

content.text: China reached it’s peak by the time Xi come to power. India has a chance of getting to closer to China in terms of potential, that is knowledge and Skill. We lack Leadership and Investment. I made guess in 2010-2012 , while I made trips to China, that it is inevitable for India to follow the China’s growth path, in 20 years to come, due to Market Potential. But we couldn’t get there.

comments.name: T Tirumala Rao
